3.1 Module 1 - SkillSpark Mobile App
FR1.1: The system shall allow child to choose between integrated games.
FR1.2: The system shall customize game content based on caregiver preferences.
FR1.3: The system shall provide interactive environment with rewards and encouraging comments.
FR1.4: The system shall analyze child's interactions to make real-time modifications reducing sensory load.
FR1.5: The system shall provide default sensory-friendly settings customizable by caregiver or child.
FR1.6: The system shall use scenarios within games to teach social responses.
FR1.7: The system shall analyze child's emotions by monitoring video and audio.

3.2 Module 2 - Virtual Pet Game
FR2.1: The system shall allow caregiver to select virtual pet according to preferences.
FR2.2: The system shall provide tasks for child to complete with virtual pet.
FR2.3: The system shall give positive feedback when child chooses correct option.
FR2.4: The system shall adjust task complexity based on child's reaction time.
FR2.5: The system shall integrate alphabet learning where child identifies letters or words.

3.3 Module 3 - Road Race Game
FR3.1: The system shall integrate math problems into the game.
FR3.2: The system shall randomly select car or select based on caregiver preferences, changing based on emotion analysis.
FR3.3: The system shall include road safety tasks (stopping at red lights).
FR3.4: The system shall provide immediate feedback on child's answers during race.
FR3.5: The system shall monitor child's performance in road safety tasks and provide feedback.
FR3.6: The system shall make changes to car in real-time based on child's emotions.

3.4 Module 4 - Caregiver's Dashboard
FR4.1: The system shall allow caregivers to monitor child's progress through dashboard.
FR4.2: The system shall allow caregiver to enter child preferences at game start for customization.
FR4.3: The system shall display child's performance details (game performance, response times, completed tasks).
FR4.4: The system shall analyze and identify emotional triggers detected during gameplay.
FR4.5: The system shall allow caregivers to adjust game complexity level through dashboard.
FR4.6: The system shall allow caregiver to make changes to user interface to reduce sensory overload.
FR4.7: The system shall apply caregiver-modified settings in real-time.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Restoration of Game
NFR1: System shall restore accurate last saved progress upon reloading.

4.2 Usability
NFR2: User-friendly interface with clear instructions and minimal helping screens.

4.3 Performance
NFR3: Games shall load within 15 seconds on devices with ≥ 4GB RAM.
NFR4: System shall respond to user inputs within seconds.

4.4 Security
NFR5: Real-time videos shall not be stored in any database to prevent unauthorized access.
NFR6: System shall notify caregiver and make immediate modifications without storing sensitive information.

4.5 Scalability
NFR7: Game shall run on all Android devices (smartphones, tablets) with adjustable graphic settings.
NFR8: Game shall handle 500 concurrent users without problems.

Project: FireCar - Autonomous Indoor Firefighting Robot
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
FireCar is an autonomous indoor firefighting robot designed for environments like server rooms and data centers where constant human supervision is not feasible, patrolling fixed areas for fire occurrence and extinguishing before spreading.

1.2 Scope
FireCar is a four-wheeled vehicle with sensors, motors, cameras, and actuators controlled by Raspberry Pi 4, featuring room mapping, obstacle avoidance, fire detection, communication via Wi-Fi, and fire suppression using CO2/water mist.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- BO: Battery Operated
- ML: Machine Learning
- GUI: Graphical User Interface

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
FireCar automates fire detection and control, reducing human involvement and risk, providing targeted suppression to minimize damage from traditional methods like sprinklers.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Camera-based room mapping
- Obstacle identification
- Path planning
- Fire detection using ML models
- Fire suppression mechanism
- User interface for monitoring and manual control
- Fire logs and reporting

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Admin/User: Monitor automatic operations, manage manual control
- Building Maintenance Team: Oversee room mapping and gas detection
- Emergency Response Team: Respond to fire or gas leak alerts
- Building Inhabitants: Rely on system's alerts for safety

2.4 Operating Environment
Embedded system (Raspberry Pi 4), web application, Wi-Fi communication.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Module 1 - Camera-Based Room Mapping and Obstacle Identification
FR1.1: The system shall create 2D map of the room using camera input and recognize all obstacles.
FR1.2: The system shall refresh the room map in real-time as new obstacles are identified.
FR1.3: The system shall actively steer clear of obstacles based on detected locations.
FR1.4: The system shall show room map including obstacles on user interface for monitoring.
FR1.5: The system shall compute and follow optimal path for navigation based on real-time map updates.

3.2 Module 2 - Fire Detection and Response Mechanisms
FR2.1: The system shall detect fire and smoke in real-time using camera and machine learning models.
FR2.2: The system shall automatically navigate to fire location, avoiding obstacles, and activate fire suppression mechanism.
FR2.3: The system shall create log entry for each fire detection and suppression event with time, location, and method.
FR2.4: The system shall enable admin to manually control fire suppression mechanism in special circumstances.
FR2.5: The system shall offer admin ability to review logs and fire events through user interface.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Reliability
NFR1: MTBF ≥ 1,000 hours in stable environment.
NFR2: System shall alert administrator and stop operations upon camera or fire detection sensor failure.

4.2 Usability
NFR3: Room map and obstacle locations accessible within three clicks.
NFR4: Admin shall manually operate fire suppression system within two clicks.
NFR5: Fire event logs retrievable in structured format with ≤ two interactions.

4.3 Performance
NFR6: Fire or smoke identification ≤ 2 seconds of appearance in camera's field of view.
NFR7: Room map refresh with updated obstacle information ≤ 1 second.
NFR8: Fire suppression commencement ≤ 5 seconds of detection; small fires extinguished within 5 minutes.

4.4 Security
NFR9: Multi-factor authentication for manual fire suppression controls access.
NFR10: All logs and event data encrypted during transmission.
NFR11: Manual control automatically disabled if security breach detected.

SOFTWARE REQUIREMENTS SPECIFICATION (IEEE 830)
Project: SignsEye - Geo-Tagging US Roadway Assets Using 2D Images
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This document specifies requirements for development of a system aimed at geo-tagging US roadway assets using 2D images, leveraging deep learning models to enhance accuracy of asset geolocation with margin of error within 3 feet.

1.2 Scope
The system integrates advanced monocular depth estimation models with geospatial data processing to achieve high precision in geolocation, processing static 2D images to predict GPS coordinates of identified assets (traffic signs, light poles).

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- GPS: Global Positioning System
- API: Application Programming Interface
- ERD: Entity-Relationship Diagram

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
The system improves precision of asset location identification, enhancing efficiency of infrastructure management and reducing resources required for asset tracking and maintenance.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Image preprocessing (deblurring, defogging, rain streak removal)
- Depth map creation using depth estimation model
- Road sign and bounding box metadata utilization
- Distance calculation from depth map
- Geo-coordinates calculation using camera geolocation
- Geo-tagging and map display

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Transportation Agency Staff: Upload images, retrieve GPS coordinates for asset locations
- Developers and Data Scientists: Maintain, upgrade, extend system
- System Administrators: Manage deployment, security, performance monitoring
- Supervisors and Project Managers: High-level progress and performance tracking

2.4 Operating Environment
Web application, Python deep learning frameworks (TensorFlow/PyTorch), cloud deployment.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Image Preprocessing
FR1.1: The system shall detect blurry images and apply deblurring techniques.
FR1.2: The system shall identify foggy images and apply defogging algorithms.
FR1.3: The system shall detect rainy images and remove water streaks using preprocessing techniques.

3.2 Depth Map Creation
FR2.1: The system shall generate depth map for each preprocessed image using depth estimation model.
FR2.2: The system shall ensure depth map corresponds to real-world dimensions of scene.

3.3 Road Sign and Bounding Box Metadata
FR3.1: The system shall use metadata to identify already detected road signs within image.
FR3.2: The system shall extract pixel values of bounding box containing road sign from depth map.

3.4 Distance Calculation
FR4.1: The system shall convert pixel values of bounding box in depth map to real-life units.
FR4.2: The system shall calculate distance between road sign and camera based on converted depth values.

3.5 Geo-Coordinates Calculation
FR5.1: The system shall use camera geolocation from metadata to calculate geo-coordinates of road sign.
FR5.2: The system shall calculate angle of road sign relative to camera position.
FR5.3: The system shall use geo-calculation algorithms to determine road sign's exact latitude and longitude.

3.6 Geo-Tagging and Display
FR6.1: The system shall attach geo-coordinates to detected road sign as metadata.
FR6.2: The system shall display geo-tagged road sign on map using Maps API.
FR6.3: The system shall allow zooming and panning functionality for map display.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Performance
NFR1: Process each image and produce geo-tagged results ≤ 5 seconds for 1920x1080 resolution.
NFR2: Process dataset of ≥ 10,000 images within 8 hours.
NFR3: Depth estimation model latency < 2 seconds per image.

4.2 Scalability
NFR4: Scale to process up to 30,000 images in one batch without significant increase in processing time.
NFR5: Horizontal scaling by adding additional processing nodes.

4.3 Reliability
NFR6: Uptime ≥ 99.9%.
NFR7: Retry mechanisms for image processing in case of failure.
NFR8: Recovery from failures within 2 minutes without data loss.

4.4 Accuracy
NFR9: Geo-coordinates calculation margin of error ≤ 3 feet.

4.5 Usability
NFR10: Map display interface ≤ 30 minutes training for new users.
NFR11: Export geo-tagged results and reports without technical expertise.
NFR12: Clear and actionable error messages.

4.6 Security
NFR13: Encryption (TLS) for all communications involving image data and geo-tagged results.
NFR14: User authentication via username and password.
NFR15: Log all user actions and changes to geo-tagging data.

4.7 Maintainability
NFR16: Standard coding practices; new developers understand codebase within 2 weeks.
NFR17: Updates and feature additions with minimal downtime using CI/CD pipeline.

4.8 Compatibility
NFR18: Integrate with multiple map APIs (Google Maps, OpenStreetMap).
NFR19: Support common image formats (JPEG, PNG, TIFF).
NFR20: Compatible with Windows and Linux operating systems.

4.9 Legal and Regulatory
NFR21: Comply with data protection regulations (GDPR).
NFR22: Obtain explicit user consent before collecting or processing geolocation data.

Project: AiUML - AI-Powered UML Diagram Generator
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
AiUML is an artificially intelligent system for automated generation of UML diagrams from textual requirements in natural language using Large Language Models, supporting software development in object-oriented systems.

1.2 Scope
AiUML covers generation of PlantUML diagrams (Class, Use Case, Sequence, Entity Relationship) using natural language processing and program synthesis techniques, intended for software engineers, system architects, project managers, and academic community.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- UML: Unified Modeling Language
- LLM: Large Language Model
- DSL: Domain-Specific Language
- NLP: Natural Language Processing

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
AiUML improves software design workflow by providing prompt visual representation of systems, improving efficiency and quality of software development artifacts.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Unrestrictive textual input
- Automated diagram generation
- AI-driven diagram optimization
- Diagram customizer and editor (DSL editor, themes, colors)
- Multi-diagram support (Class, Use Case, Sequence, ERD)
- Export diagrams in multiple formats (SVG, PNG)
- Session and diagram code storage
- User-friendly interface

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Software Engineers: Proficient in software development, need quick reliable UML diagrams
- System Architects: High-level system design, need well-structured diagrams
- Project Managers: Need high-level overviews for tracking progress
- Testers/QA Engineers: Need UML diagrams for developing test cases

- Documentation Writers: Need clear, concise visual diagrams
- Business Analysts: Need visual representations for stakeholder communication

Web application, client-server architecture, MongoDB for storage.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Unrestrictive Textual Requirements Input
FR1.1: Users shall enter text requirements in any manner using natural language.
FR1.2: System shall support user stories, functional requirements, case studies, software specifications.
FR1.3: System shall identify ambiguous textual requirements during input processing.
FR1.4: System shall detect anaphoric ambiguities within textual requirements.
FR1.5: System shall provide mechanisms to resolve detected ambiguities.

3.2 Automated Diagram Generation
FR2.1: System shall generate code for diagrams based on natural language textual input.
FR2.2: System shall automatically render diagrams on screen after generating code.
FR2.3: System shall provide zoom in and zoom out options for rendered diagrams.

3.3 AI Driven Diagram Optimization
FR3.1: System shall generate well-structured, easy-to-understand diagrams per software design standards.
FR3.2: System shall model complex relationships and produce accurate UML diagrams.
FR3.3: System shall apply incremental changes based on user input while retaining context.
FR3.4: System shall handle iterative modification requests preserving structure and context.

3.4 Diagram Customizer and Editor
FR4.1: Users shall edit diagram code directly in DSL editor.
FR4.2: System shall allow alteration of diagram elements and structure using DSL editor.
FR4.3: System shall provide customization options for applying different colors.
FR4.4: Users shall choose and apply different themes to diagrams.
FR4.5: System shall instantly render modified diagrams as changes are made.

3.5 Multi Diagram Support
FR5.1: System shall generate wide variety of diagram kinds.
FR5.2: Users shall generate Class Diagrams using textual requirements.
FR5.3: Users shall generate Use Case Diagrams using textual requirements.
FR5.4: Users shall generate Sequence Diagrams using textual requirements.
FR5.5: Users shall generate Entity Relationship Diagrams using textual requirements.

3.6 Exporting Diagrams in Multiple Formats
FR6.1: System shall provide multiple export options including SVG and PNG.
FR6.2: System shall export diagrams in high-quality scalable vector graphics (SVG) format.
FR6.3: System shall allow users to easily download exported diagrams.

3.7 Session and Diagram Code Storage
FR7.1: System shall store user's previous sessions.
FR7.2: System shall save previous diagrams and corresponding code.
FR7.3: System shall store diagram codes in PlantUML format.
FR7.4: System shall maintain user workspaces by storing session data.
FR7.5: Users shall access and edit previously stored diagrams.

3.8 User Friendly Interface
FR8.1: System shall provide comprehensive web-based platform integrating all modules.
FR8.2: System shall offer minimalistic, easy-to-navigate interface.
FR8.3: System shall provide real-time feedback and results in response to user input.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Reliability
NFR1: MTBF ≥ 500 hours.
NFR2: System reset within 30 seconds while keeping data saved upon failure.
NFR3: Proper user notification and retry options for diagram generation failure.
NFR4: Restore last stable state upon system failure.

4.2 Usability
NFR5: Diagram creation within three clicks.
NFR6: User-friendly interface for all users.
NFR7: Instructions to reduce user errors by 30%.

4.3 Performance
NFR8: Parse textual input and produce diagram ≤ 10 seconds.
NFR9: Process at least 50 user queries simultaneously without lag.
NFR10: Respond to diagram edits within 5 seconds.
NFR11: Respond to optimization requests within 5 seconds.

4.4 Security
NFR12: Authentication to protect system and previous sessions.
NFR13: Protection for personal and session data up to 90%.

Project: ScholarWatch - AI-Powered Learning Management System
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This document outlines requirements for ScholarWatch, an AI-driven Learning Management System that integrates with Moodle to enhance teaching and learning by providing instructors with insights into student engagement and performance.

1.2 Scope
ScholarWatch features include real-time engagement tracking (gaze tracking, posture detection, facial expression recognition), automated assessment creation (personalized MCQ quizzes), activity monitoring (anti-cheating mechanisms), post-assessment adaptation, content creation tools, attendance monitoring, and insights analysis dashboard.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- LMS: Learning Management System
- AI: Artificial Intelligence
- MLP: Multi-Layer Perceptron
- API: Application Programming Interface

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
Scholarwatch simplifies course management and improves overall learning experience by providing intelligent tools for monitoring student engagement, tracking progress, and offering personalized content.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Real-time engagement tracking (gaze, posture, facial expressions)
- Automated assessment creation (MCQ quizzes)
- Activity monitoring (tab-switch, copy-paste, split-screen detection)
- Post-assessment adaptation
- Content creation tools (automated lecture slides)
- Attendance monitoring based on attentiveness
- Insights analysis dashboard

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Instructors: Create and manage content, track student progress, evaluate performance
- Students: Access course materials, take quizzes, track progress
- Researchers: Analyze student engagement data, learning outcomes

2.4 Operating Environment
Moodle-integrated platform, web application.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 User Attention Module
FR1.1: The system shall use gaze tracking, posture (laying, slouching), and facial expression recognition (yawning, drowsy) to monitor student attention.
FR1.2: Student attentiveness shall be monitored against attentiveness threshold set by instructor.

3.2 Insights Module
FR2.1: The system shall check gaze periods on specific slide areas to identify areas requiring extra focus.
FR2.2: The instructor shall tag lectures as prerequisites for other lectures/topics.
FR2.3: The instructor shall view class progress with filters (top scorers, below average, non-attentive).
FR2.4: The student shall access individual dashboard showing attention span, weak areas, attendance, scores, progress.

3.3 Generation Module
FR3.1: Instructor shall upload PDF of chapter/topic or upload own PPT slides.
FR3.2: Instructor shall receive PPT slides (textual only) from uploaded PDF with checkboxes for theme, lines per slide, total slides.
FR3.3: Instructor shall tag lectures as prerequisites.
FR3.4: Instructor shall create quizzes from chosen lectures/slides.
FR3.5: Check boxes for number of questions and multiple choices per quiz.

3.4 Activity Monitoring Module
FR4.1: The system shall monitor student screen during quizzes.
FR4.2: The system shall detect tab switching, split-screen windows, and gaze tracking to identify potential cheating.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Reliability
NFR1: Uptime ≥ 90%, 24/7 availability for courses, quizzes, real-time tracking.

4.2 Performance
NFR2: Real-time student attention data processing with minimal latency.
NFR3: Quiz generation ≤ 10 seconds.
NFR4: Quiz grading ≤ 5 seconds.

4.3 Scalability
NFR5: Support up to 1,000,000 concurrent users.
NFR6: Handle up to 50,000 courses with up to 5,000 users per course.
NFR7: Accommodate up to 50 roles and 100 course categories nested up to 10 levels.

4.4 Compliance
NFR8: Comply with educational data privacy laws; all personal and academic data handled securely.

Project: SkillBazaar - Blue Collar Job Marketplace
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
SkillBazaar revolutionizes the blue collar job marketplace by integrating automation and AI to build a platform where illiterate people can easily sell their services, decreasing unemployment caused by informal networks of word-of-mouth.

1.2 Scope
SkillBazaar is a bilingual platform empowering illiterate and poor blue collar workers (gardeners, plumbers, electricians, daily wage laborers, women in handicraft) with features including automated profile building using CNIC and voice input, job postings, bookings, ratings, multilingual support, voice-based conversational bot, geo-location services, voice-based searches, and user analytics.

- OCR: Optical Character Recognition
- ASR: Automatic Speech Recognition
- AI: Artificial Intelligence
- UI: User Interface

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
SkillBazaar automates the blue collar job marketplace, allowing daily wage workers to get profiles created and find suitable jobs with minimal input.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Automated profile creation using OCR (CNIC) and voice input
- Multilingual support (English, Urdu)
- Conversational bot with ASR technology
- Service/job posting
- Service/job matching using AI algorithm
- Profile matching and maintenance
- Geo-location services
- Booking and confirmations
- Rating and feedback system
- In-app messaging

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Blue Collar Worker: Daily wage worker offering labor-intensive services, limited literacy, prefers voice input
- Employers: Individuals, businesses, organizations needing blue collar workers
- Job Seekers: Skilled people wanting to sell services
- Non-Literate Users: Cannot read/write English, need multilingual and voice support

2.4 Operating Environment
Mobile application (Android/iOS), backend services with AI matching algorithm.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Profile Creation
FR1.1: English text detection from CNIC using OCR.
FR1.2: Urdu text detection from CNIC using OCR.
FR1.3: Speech recognition and understanding.
FR1.4: Extraction of important information from voice input.
FR1.5: Retrieved information stored in database.

3.2 Multilingual Support
FR2.1: Support for English language.
FR2.2: Support for Urdu language.

3.3 Conversational Bot
FR3.1: Speech recognition and understanding.
FR3.2: Urdu voice-based conversation and correct interpretation.
FR3.3: Quick and easy-to-understand replies.

3.4 Service/Job Posting and Matching
FR4.1: Services posted by workers seeking jobs.
FR4.2: Jobs posted by people looking for blue collar workers.
FR4.3: Application shall manage both types of postings.
FR4.4: AI algorithm shall match services and jobs according to user/worker preferences.
FR4.5: Geo-location shall be matched along with other preferences.
FR4.6: Voice-based conversational bot for this module.

3.5 Profile Matching and Maintenance
FR5.1: Profile matching based on rating, reviews, geo-location, and other preferences.
FR5.2: Profile matching allows voice input.
FR5.3: System shall manage profiles for workers to add new information.

4.1 Reliability

NFR2: Recovery and resume normal operations within 2 minutes after failure detection.
NFR3: Error detection mechanisms for failed job postings or profile updates, notify user within 5 seconds.
NFR4: Store incomplete user sessions and restore upon next login.

NFR6: Multilingual navigation with options for Urdu and English, switch languages within 1 click.
NFR7: Job posting ≤ 1 minute via simple form or voice command.

NFR9: Clear on-screen guidance and error recovery prompts for invalid inputs.

Project: DigiTransacFraudDetect - Credit Card Fraud Detection using GNN
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This document outlines development of a software system for credit card fraud detection using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with a novel encoder-decoder architecture, mapping connections between customers and merchants to improve fraud detection accuracy.

1.2 Scope
The system generates synthetic datasets mimicking real-life transaction scenarios, performs feature engineering, constructs graph-based models (customers and merchants as nodes, transactions as edges), trains GNN-based fraud detector, implements batch normalization for faster training, provides user-friendly interface with visualization and dashboards, and enables integration with existing banking systems.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- GNN: Graph Neural Network
- ML: Machine Learning
- CSV: Comma-Separated Values
- API: Application Programming Interface

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
The system uses GNN-based approach to learn complex patterns within transaction data, leading to higher fraud detection rates while being scalable for real-time fraud detection.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Synthetic data generation
- Feature engineering
- Data cleaning and preprocessing
- Graph generation (customers, merchants as nodes; transactions as edges)
- GNN model training and evaluation
- Real-time fraudulent transaction detection
- Batch processing for manually uploaded transactions
- Fraud detection reports
- Dashboard with performance metrics

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Financial Institution Employees: Monitor flagged transactions, respond effectively (≈200 users)
- System Administrators: Manage backend systems, databases, integrations (10-15 administrators)
- Fraud Detection Software Developers: Modify/adapt system for unique requirements (≈50 developers)
- Executives and Regulatory Auditors: View performance dashboards and reports (10-20 senior users)

2.4 Operating Environment
Web application, backend processing, real-time streaming capabilities.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Module 1 - Synthetic Data Generation and Preprocessing
FR1.1: The system shall generate synthetic transaction data including transaction amount, time, customer ID, merchant ID, and fraud labels.
FR1.2: The system shall support generation of data at different scales mimicking real-world transaction volumes.
FR1.3: The system shall generate fraudulent and non-fraudulent transactions in proportions reflecting real-world data.
FR1.4: The system shall clean and preprocess generated synthetic data, removing invalid or inconsistent entries.
FR1.5: The system shall normalize transaction data to ensure consistency across all features.
FR1.6: The system shall split dataset into training, validation, and test sets.

3.2 Module 2 - Graph Generation and GNN Model Training
FR2.1: The system shall convert transaction data into graph structure (customers and merchants as nodes, transactions as edges).
FR2.2: The system shall ensure graph generation process scales to handle large transaction datasets efficiently.
FR2.3: The system shall visualize graph structure for easier understanding of relationships.
FR2.4: The system shall implement encoder-decoder Graph Neural Network to train on graph-structured data.
FR2.5: The system shall integrate batch normalization techniques to optimize GNN training.
FR2.6: The system shall fine-tune GNN hyperparameters (learning rate, epochs) to maximize fraud detection accuracy.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Reliability
NFR1: MTBF ≥ 1,000 hours.
NFR2: Automatic recovery attempt within 5 minutes upon failure.
NFR3: Continuous monitoring; users notified within 30 seconds of failure.
NFR4: Preservation of transaction data and automatic resumption from failure point.

4.2 Usability

NFR6: New user complete fraud investigation tasks without training within 1 hour.
NFR7: Real-time visual feedback (loading indicators, transaction status).
NFR8: Clear, actionable error messages with steps to resolve within 10 seconds.

4.3 Performance
NFR9: Process up to 1,000 transactions per second (TPS) in real-time.
NFR10: GNN model detect fraudulent transactions with response time ≤ 1 minute per transaction.
NFR11: Maximum latency of 10 seconds for real-time dashboard updates.
NFR12: Automatic scaling to handle 5x normal load without manual intervention.

NFR14: Multi-factor authentication for all users accessing critical components.
NFR15: Auto logout after 10 minutes of inactivity.

SOFTWARE REQUIREMENTS SPECIFICATION (IEEE 830)
Project: Mal-Assf - Malware Analysis using API Sequence Semantic Fusion
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Problem Statement
Malware complexity is growing; classic static analysis approaches often fail to address new threats due to obfuscation, polymorphism, and packing techniques. Current dynamic analysis tools have scalability and accuracy issues in understanding API call sequences critical for studying malware behavior.

1.2 Scope
Mal-Assf is a desktop application for analyzing and detecting malicious binary executables using dynamic analysis, API sequence analysis, and semantic fusion. Features include dataset collection (MalwareBazaar, VirusTotal), sandbox execution, API sequence extraction, API2Vec for vectorization, BiLSTM for sequence analysis, attention mechanism, MLP classifier, and comprehensive report generation.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- API: Application Programming Interface
- BiLSTM: Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory
- MLP: Multi-Layer Perceptron
- GNN: Graph Neural Network

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
Mal-Assf provides a multi-tier malware analysis and detection framework using dynamic analysis, API sequence analysis, and semantic fusion to determine malicious actions.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Data collection from MalwareBazaar, VirusTotal, Cuckoo Sandbox
- Data cleaning, tagging, pre-processing
- Sandbox execution in isolated environment
- API sequence extraction preserving timing context
- API2Vec for vectorizing API calls
- BiLSTM for sequence pattern analysis
- Attention mechanism for focusing on relevant sequence parts
- MLP classifier for malware/benign classification
- Comprehensive report generation with behavior details

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Security Analysts: Monitor and analyze malware detection results
- Users: Upload files, receive analysis reports
- System Administrators: Manage deployment, sandbox environment, classifiers
- Developers: Improve malware detection system components

2.4 Operating Environment
Desktop application, sandbox environment, machine learning models.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Module 1 - Data Collection and Sandbox
FR1.1: The system shall collect datasets containing malware and benign samples from sources like MalwareBazaar.
FR1.2: The system shall perform data cleaning, tagging, and preliminary pre-processing of collected samples.
FR1.3: The system shall execute binary files in an isolated sandbox environment to track system behavior.

3.2 Module 2 - API Sequence Extraction, Semantic Analysis, and Classification
FR2.1: The system shall capture and record API call sequences produced by binary file execution.
FR2.2: The system shall preserve timing context of API calls for accurate sequence analysis.
FR2.3: The system shall employ API2Vec to convert API calls into vector forms for semantic evaluation.
FR2.4: The system shall use BiLSTM to identify patterns in API sequences and attention model to highlight relevant portions.
FR2.5: The system shall classify API sequences as malicious or benign using MLP classifier.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Reliability
NFR1: MTBF ≥ 100 hours during normal operation.
NFR2: 99.9% uptime for malware analysis functionality.

4.2 Usability
NFR3: Upload and analyze files with ≤ 3 interactions.
NFR4: Clear feedback and error messages for file upload and report generation.

4.3 Performance
NFR5: Process and analyze binary executables up to 10MB within 5 minutes.
NFR6: Support parallel execution of analysis tasks for multiple users.

4.4 Security
NFR7: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) for sensitive components.
NFR8: Uploaded files quarantined and securely isolated.
NFR9: Data transmission encrypted using SSL/TLS.
NFR10: All user activities logged for audit trail.

4.5 Scalability
NFR11: Handle scalable number of simultaneous file analysis requests (up to 100 concurrent users).
NFR12: Support expansion for larger datasets and additional machine learning models.

FR2.10: The system shall allow admin and residents to view their vote.
FR2.11: The system shall calculate accurate voting results.
FR2.12: The system shall allow admin to announce voting results.
FR2.13: The system shall conclude voting results and make available to residents.

3.3 Shared Resources
FR3.1: The system shall allow residents to list resources for community use.
FR3.2: The system shall validate fields in resource form.
FR3.3: The system shall store details in database.
FR3.4: The system shall notify admin to approve resource listing.
FR3.5: The system shall list resources on admin approval.
FR3.6: The system shall allow residents to search for resources based on required criteria.
FR3.7: The system shall display search results.
FR3.8: The system shall allow residents to view details of each resource.
FR3.9: The system shall allow residents to save resources.
FR3.10: The system shall provide filters for easy search.
FR3.11: The system shall allow residents to book a resource.
FR3.12: The system shall display resource details and allow residents to review.
FR3.13: The system shall allow residents to decide booking period.
FR3.14: The system shall notify admin to review and approve resource.

3.4 Event Management
FR4.1: The system shall validate user credentials during login.
FR4.2: The system shall display available venues, event dates, and options for booking.
FR4.3: The system shall check and confirm availability of selected venues.
FR4.4: The system shall send booking confirmation to user upon successful reservation.
FR4.5: The system shall suggest alternate time slots or nearby venues if selected option partially available.
FR4.6: The system shall notify user if no venues available for selected date and suggest other options.
FR4.7: The system shall store user event preferences for future recommendations.
FR4.8: The system shall display event recommendations based on user preferences.
FR4.9: The system shall suggest promotional events if no personalized recommendations available.
FR4.10: The system shall notify users if recommended event fully booked or canceled and provide similar alternatives.

3.5 Community News and Announcements
FR5.1: The system shall validate user credentials during login.
FR5.2: The system shall display announcements with full content, time, date, attachments.
FR5.3: The system shall optionally enable comments section for user feedback.
FR5.4: The system shall continue displaying previous announcement until new one posted.
FR5.5: The system shall display list of available members to message.
FR5.6: The system shall allow users to send, receive, and store messages, confirming successful delivery.
FR5.7: The system shall queue messages if recipient offline.
FR5.8: The system shall notify sender if recipient not available or deactivated account.

3.6 Maintenance
FR6.1: The system shall display form for users to submit maintenance queries or incident reports.
FR6.2: The system shall assign tracking number to submitted queries and notify users of successful submission.
FR6.3: The system shall validate any attached images or files and include with query submission.
FR6.4: The system shall notify users if query submission fails due to network or system errors and allow retry.
FR6.5: The system shall display current status of all submitted queries.
FR6.6: The system shall provide detailed progress updates ("in progress," "awaiting materials," "completed").
FR6.7: The system shall provide estimated completion times and reasons for delays if queries take longer.
FR6.8: The system shall allow users to provide feedback on resolved queries or reopen if issue persists.

3.7 User Authentication and Role Management
FR7.1: The system shall allow new users to register by entering name, email address, and password.
FR7.2: Users shall access system by entering password and registered email address.
FR7.3: Users shall reset password using "Forgotten Password" option.
FR7.4: The system shall validate fields for strong password (length, uppercase, numbers, special characters).
FR7.5: The system shall grant access to specific functionalities based on assigned role.
FR7.6: Admin shall add, modify, and remove user roles through system.
FR7.7: Sensitive modules and admin functions not accessible by non-admin users.
FR7.8: The system shall permit administrator to edit user accounts (password resets, account cancellation).
FR7.9: The system shall maintain log activity, especially at admin level.
FR7.10: The system shall provide role-based permissions (polling generation, list properties, access admin dashboard).

3.8 Admin Dashboard
FR8.1: Admin view shall provide overview dashboard showing activity and participation (active residents, events, property listings, maintenance requests).
FR8.2: The system shall supply emerging messages, notifications, or issues reported in real-time.
FR8.3: The system shall allow admin to sort and filter dashboard based on activity type, date, or specific users.
FR8.4: The system shall provide rapid access to important modules (voting polls, event management, property operations).
FR8.5: Critical activities (outstanding maintenance issues, upcoming voting dates) displayed on site.
FR8.6: The system shall have resource-sharing and property listings pending approval.
FR8.7: The system shall allow admin to export reports in PDF, Excel, or other file types.
FR8.8: The system shall enable admin to grant selective rights and switch user roles.
FR8.9: Admin shall search citizens using parameters like name, email, role, or account status.
FR8.10: The system shall enable community manager to add or remove members from group.
FR8.11: The system shall enable admin to provide specific permissions and switch user roles.
FR8.12: The system shall allow administrator to suspend or deactivate user accounts permanently or temporarily.
FR8.13: The system shall allow administrator to reset user passwords and alert them of change.
FR8.14: The system shall maintain audit log of administrator actions (role changes, account deactivation, password resets).

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Reliability
NFR1: MTBF ≥ 1,000 hours.
NFR2: System unavailable due to error in functionality (property listing, voting, resource sharing, communication) defined as failure.
NFR3: Backup mechanism to prevent single failures.
NFR4: Continuous log monitoring for error detection.
NFR5: Automated backup mechanism to recover single operation within 5 minutes.

4.2 Usability
NFR6: Users learn to use within 10 minutes.
NFR7: User-friendly interface with minimal effort.
NFR8: Retrieve property listing with single interaction.
NFR9: Step-by-step guide for new property purchase process.
NFR10: Error message display for incorrect or invalid details.

4.3 Performance
NFR11: System response to user requests within 3 seconds.
NFR12: Handle user increases without degradation.
NFR13: Handle peak loads.
NFR14: Use resources (RAM, CPU, GPU) efficiently.
NFR15: 90% of web pages load within 4 seconds over ≥ 20 Mbps internet.
NFR16: Handle at least 500 residents without performance degradation.
NFR17: Handle at least 50 transactions during peak time.

4.4 Security
NFR18: Encrypt sensitive data and secure in blockchain.
NFR19: Strong authorization and authentication methods (2FA, MFA).
NFR20: Role-based access control.
NFR21: Audit all transactions and trail-related activities.
NFR22: Standard algorithms to protect sensitive information.
NFR23: Multi-factor authentication for admin login.
NFR24: Maintain user logs with at least 6 months audit details.

SOFTWARE REQUIREMENTS SPECIFICATION (IEEE 830)
Project: Syntra - Compliance Automation Tool (ISO 27001)
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Problem Statement
Compliance management remains largely manual process globally, resulting in inefficiency, error, and delay, increasing risk of missing critical standards like ISO 27001. Compliance managers depend on spreadsheets, emails, and surveys, which are time-consuming and prone to human error. Existing solutions are expensive, inaccessible to smaller organizations.

1.2 Scope
Syntra automates key compliance tasks including control assignment, evidence collection, gap and risk analysis, and reporting for ISO 27001 standards. Organizations register, input domain, personnel, and services, receiving customized Statement of Applicability (SoA). Technical controls validated through MDM technology; non-technical controls by submitting evidence. Tool automates control assignments, sends email notifications, provides interface for evidence submission, triggers mitigation plans for non-compliance, and generates executive and technical reports.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- SoA: Statement of Applicability
- MDM: Mobile Device Management
- LLM: Large Language Model
- RBAC: Role-Based Access Control

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
Syntra reduces time, effort, and errors in achieving compliance, making process more cost-effective for organizations.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Organization registration
- Customized Statement of Applicability (SoA) generation
- Control assignment (drag-and-drop)
- Email notifications to control handlers
- Evidence submission interface
- Gap and risk assessment
- Mitigation plan triggering
- Executive and technical report generation
- Admin panel for standards and user management
- Model server for evidence validation

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Compliance Manager: Oversee compliance initiatives, assign controls, track progress
- Control Handler: Submit evidence, complete gap assessments
- Admin: Manage system, update standards, control management
- Tester: Test software on requirements
- Developers: Develop, maintain, implement compliance tool
- Auditors/Reviewers: Examine reports, verify evidence and controls

2.4 Operating Environment
Web application, client-server architecture, cloud storage.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Module 1 - Controller
FR1.1: The system shall enable compliance managers to create and manage compliance campaigns.
FR1.2: The system shall enable control owners, custodians, and testers assignment by compliance managers.
FR1.3: The system shall have interactive interface for selection of controls.
FR1.4: The system shall automate sending alerts/notifications and reminders to custodians and control owners.
FR1.5: The system shall have interface for evidence submission with proper validation checks.
FR1.6: The system shall allow submission of evidence by control handlers to assigned controls.
FR1.7: The system shall enable mitigation phase in event of non-compliance identified.

3.2 Module 2 - Evidence Analyzer Module
FR2.1: The system shall accept submitted evidence and validate against chosen compliance standards (ISO 27001).
FR2.2: The system shall perform real-time gap assessments from submitted evidence.
FR2.3: The system shall analyze evidence about compliance and return compliance level.
FR2.4: The system shall trigger mitigation phase with recommendations for action.
FR2.5: The system shall display status on submitted evidence.

3.3 Module 3 - Report Generation
FR3.1: Technical reports for closer look at compliance status.
FR3.2: The system shall produce executive reports summarizing total compliance status.
FR3.3: The system shall export reports in different formats (PDF, CSV).

3.4 Module 4 - Admin Panel Module
FR4.1: The system shall support adding, updating, deletion of compliance controls based on standards changes.
FR4.2: The system shall have admin approvals control with prior standard before storing in database.
FR4.3: The system shall provide admin feature to review and validate results from model server.
FR4.4: The system shall allow admin on user accounts management (create, update, deactivate user roles).

3.5 Module 5 - Model Server Module
FR5.1: Interface with other modules so compliance status triggers further activities.
FR5.2: The system shall validate evidence and give output through feedback using input evidence.
FR5.3: The system shall provide output giving feedback relating to controls and evidence needed through standards input.
FR5.4: The system shall analyze evidence submission made and determine if follow-up action required.
FR5.5: The system shall extract pertinent controls, evidence requirements, and other information from compliance standards (ISO 27001) using LLM models.

3.6 Module 6 - Storage Module
FR6.1: The system shall ensure secure file storage of evidence files submitted by user.
FR6.2: The system shall allow retrieval of stored evidence files.
FR6.3: The system shall ensure version control of evidence files to track changes.
FR6.4: The system shall have data encryption in all stored evidence files.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Reliability
NFR1: MTBF ≥ 1,000 hours.
NFR2: Automatic recovery facility to restore last known stable state within 5 minutes upon failure.
NFR3: Record all errors and failures in error log.
NFR4: Error detection policy notifying administrators of failures within 2 minutes.

4.2 Usability
NFR5: Compliance managers accomplish tasks through ≤ 3 clicks.
NFR6: Drag-and-drop interface for evidence submission.
NFR7: Contextual help and tooltips on all user interface elements.
NFR8: WCAG 2.1 Level AA accessibility standards.

4.3 Performance
NFR9: 95% of evidence submissions processed and validation results reported < 5 seconds.
NFR10: Support ≥ 100 concurrent users without degradation.
NFR11: Compliance reports generated < 10 seconds for average requests.
NFR12: Response time < 3 seconds for all user interface actions.

4.4 Security
NFR13: Restrict access to highly classified information with 99.9% security against unauthorized access.
NFR14: Multi-factor authentication for all users.
NFR15: Protect all sensitive data through industry-standard encryption algorithms in transit and at rest.
NFR16: Quarterly security audits of vulnerability.

SOFTWARE REQUIREMENTS SPECIFICATION (IEEE 830)
Project: FontFusion - Raster to Vector Font Converter
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This document outlines the problem, motivation, and proposed solution for FontFusion, a project aimed at converting outdated raster fonts into modern, scalable vector fonts. The solution automates feature extraction, classification, and matching to find suitable vector font equivalents.

1.2 Scope
The project's goal is to compare raster fonts with vector fonts, classify them by size, shape, and style, and predict font outlines. It aims to solve the visual and resolution issues of raster fonts on high-resolution screens by converting them to similar, scalable vector fonts.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- TTF: TrueType Font
- OTF: OpenType Font
- CNN: Convolutional Neural Network
- SVM: Support Vector Machine

1.4 References
- [33] Application and Difference of Raster and Vector Graphics
- [44] TrueType Transformer: Character and Font Style Recognition in Outline Format
- [11] Large-Scale Font Identification from Document Images
- [55] DeepFont: Identify Your Font from An Image
- [22] Raster-to-Vector: Revisiting Floorplan Transformation

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
The system is developed to assist a client company in transitioning from old-fashioned raster fonts to modern vector fonts, maintaining visual consistency.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Feature extraction from raster and vector fonts.
- Classification of fonts based on style, shape, and size.
- Matching raster fonts to a library of TTF/OTF fonts.
- A web interface for users to upload images and view the best vector font match.

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Company Client: The primary stakeholder utilizing the system to modernize their fonts.
- End-User: Users who will interact with the web interface to upload raster font images and receive vector font suggestions.

2.4 Operating Environment
Web-based application accessible via a modern browser.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Font Feature Extraction and Preprocessing
FR1.1: The system shall generate raster font data programmatically using Python scripts.
FR1.2: The system shall render characters at multiple resolutions and styles (bold, italic).
FR1.3: The system shall apply data augmentations (rotation, noise) to ensure model robustness.
FR1.4: The system shall parse TTF/OTF files to retrieve control points and outlines.
FR1.5: The system shall extract features such as aspect ratio, stroke width, and curve smoothness.

3.2 Classification of Fonts
FR2.1: The system shall use a supervised machine learning algorithm (SVM/CNN) to classify fonts.
FR2.2: The system shall train the classifier on labeled font datasets (type, size, style, shape).
FR2.3: The system shall evaluate model performance using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

3.3 Font Prediction Model
FR3.1: The system shall define a similarity metric (Euclidean distance) to compare font feature vectors.
FR3.2: The system shall implement a heuristic-based font matching algorithm to recommend the closest vector font.

3.4 Website Development
FR4.1: The system shall allow users to upload raster font images (PNG, JPEG).
FR4.2: The system shall process uploaded images to isolate characters and remove noise.
FR4.3: The system shall display the predicted vector font results to the user.
FR4.4: The system shall provide additional information about the matched font (family, style, characteristics).

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Performance Requirements
NFR1: The system shall give real-time results for font comparison.
NFR2: The system shall efficiently handle a large number of fonts in its library.

4.2 Usability Requirements
NFR3: The system shall offer an easy interface without technical requirements from the user.

4.3 Scalability Requirements
NFR4: The system shall be upgradable to accommodate more fonts inputs and comparisons.

Project: Sentinel AI - AI-Driven Surveillance System
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This document specifies the requirements for Sentinel AI, an AI-driven intruder detection system designed to enhance existing CCTV infrastructure without costly hardware upgrades.

1.2 Scope
The project aims to develop an AI-driven intruder detection system that integrates facial recognition, liveness detection, and categorizes individuals into household members, frequent visitors, and intruders. It provides real-time alerts and can contact emergency services via an AI-driven response system. It will recognize faces even if partially covered.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- CCTV: Closed-Circuit Television
- AI: Artificial Intelligence
- MTBF: Mean Time Between Failures
- RBAC: Role-Based Access Control

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
Sentinel AI is a Software as a Service (SAAS) product that enhances existing CCTV networks by providing smart notifications and AI-enabled emergency calling.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Face recognition from various angles and with partial coverings.
- Liveliness detection to prevent spoofing.
- Intruder detection and real-time alerts.
- Automated calling to owners and emergency services.

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Homeowner: Primary user responsible for setting up and managing security features.
- Visitors: Frequent guests with limited access, recognized by the system.
- Emergency Services: External responders who receive alerts from the system.

2.4 Operating Environment
Integrates with existing CCTV camera networks; includes web and mobile applications.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Face Recognition Module
FR1.1: The system shall detect faces from various angles (front, left, right).
FR1.2: The system shall perform facial recognition despite changes in hairstyle or partial face covering (mask, muffler).
FR1.3: The system shall include liveliness detection to ensure the face is real.
FR1.4: The system shall allow the owner to add known personnel’s faces to the database.
FR1.5: The system shall trigger an intruder alert for unfamiliar faces and log the event.

3.2 AI Caller Module
FR2.1: The system shall automatically notify the owner via notifications when an intruder is detected.
FR2.2: The system shall automatically contact emergency services in case of a confirmed intruder or emergency.

3.3 Web/App Development Module
FR3.1: The system shall provide a client web app for the owner to monitor real-time video feeds and review past events.
FR3.2: The system shall provide a client mobile app with similar functionality, enabling on-the-go alerts.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Reliability
NFR1: The system shall have an MTBF of no less than 1000 hours.

4.2 Usability
NFR2: Users shall retrieve previous incident alerts with a single interaction.
NFR3: The system shall be accessible without extensive training.

4.3 Performance
NFR4: 95% of intruder alerts shall be processed within 2 seconds of detection.
NFR5: The system shall support a minimum of 100 concurrent users without performance degradation.

4.4 Security
NFR6: The effort to compromise the system shall be sufficiently high to deter unauthorized access.
NFR7: The system shall regularly audit user access and alert administrators of suspicious activity.

Project: FearLESS
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This mid evaluation report defines the scope, system-level requirements, and first iteration deliverables for FearLESS, a VR-based application helping people overcome fears of heights (Acrophobia) and darkness (Nyctophobia) using VR, fear intensity gauging, and therapist/psychiatrist counseling connections.

1.2 Scope
FearLESS uses Virtual Reality Exposure Therapy (VRET) to help users confirm and overcome irrational fears/phobias. Users take questionnaires to determine fear severity, view simulations at varying intensities, and connect with therapists. Therapists can access simulations for exposure therapy from their offices/clinics.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- VR: Virtual Reality
- VRET: Virtual Reality Exposure Therapy
- CBT: Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy
- XR: Extended Reality

1.4 References
- National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) [3]
- Cleveland Clinic [4, 8]

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
FearLESS addresses the problem that only 23% of people with phobias seek treatment. The application allows users to expose themselves to fears via simulations at varying intensities until they can conquer their reactions.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Phobia selection (Acrophobia, Nyctophobia)
- Simulation levels (easy, medium, hard)
- Professional counseling (text messaging, video chat)
- Intensity confirmation questionnaire
- Progress tracking questionnaire
- Payment module for therapy sessions
- User profile management
- Feedback system

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Acrophobic Users: Seek exposure therapy for fear of heights
- Nyctophobic Users: Seek exposure therapy for fear of darkness
- Inquisitive Users: Curious about whether they have a fear
- Counselors (Therapists/Psychiatrists): Provide exposure therapy using simulations
- VR Enthusiasts: Use application only for VR experience

2.4 Operating Environment
Desktop application with Oculus Rift headset support, Unity development environment.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Phobia Selection
FR1.1: The system shall allow user access to nyctophobia simulation.
FR1.2: The system shall allow user access to acrophobia simulation.
FR1.3: The system shall allow therapist access to both simulations.

3.2 Simulation Levels
FR2.1: The system shall allow user access to easy level for acrophobia.
FR2.2: The system shall allow user access to medium level for acrophobia.
FR2.3: The system shall allow user access to hard level for acrophobia.
FR2.4: The system shall allow user access to easy level for nyctophobia.
FR2.5: The system shall allow user access to medium level for nyctophobia.
FR2.6: The system shall allow user access to hard level for nyctophobia.

3.3 Counseling
FR3.1: The system shall allow user to chat with appointed therapist.
FR3.2: The system shall allow user to video call with appointed therapist.
FR3.3: The system shall assign users to therapists.
FR3.4: The system shall allow therapist to view client's intensity test results.
FR3.5: The system shall allow therapist to allow/deny client access to simulations.
FR3.6: The system shall allow user to request therapist change.
FR3.7: The system shall allow user to request therapist appointment.

3.4 Intensity Confirmation
FR4.1: The system shall allow user to take questionnaire before simulation.
FR4.2: The system shall allow user to view intensity confirmation results.

3.5 Progress Tracking
FR5.1: The system shall allow registered user to take questionnaire after simulation.
FR5.2: The system shall update fear intensity level based on progress questionnaire.
FR5.3: The system shall change simulation level based on fear intensity.

3.6 User Profile Management
FR6.1: The system shall allow user to create an account.
FR6.2: The system shall allow user to login using email/username and password.
FR6.3: The system shall allow user to login using Google account.
FR6.4: The system shall allow user to view profile.
FR6.5: The system shall allow user to update profile.
FR6.6: The system shall allow password reset.

3.7 Payment
FR7.1: The system shall allow therapist to set payment details.
FR7.2: The system shall allow user to set payment details.
FR7.3: The system shall allow user to pay using subscription.

3.8 Feedback
FR8.1: The system shall allow user to give application feedback.
FR8.2: The system shall allow user to provide feedback to change therapist.
FR8.3: The system shall allow user to view therapist feedback.
FR8.4: The system shall allow user to view application feedback.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Organizational Requirements
NFR1: The system shall be developed using Unity version 2023.2.7f1 LTS.
NFR2: The system shall be developed in C#.
NFR3: VR setup shall use XR Interaction Toolkit 2.5.2 and XR Plugin Management 4.4.0.

4.2 Security Requirements
NFR4: User authentication and authorization shall ensure authorized access.
NFR5: Secure password recovery mechanisms shall be provided.

4.3 Usability Requirements
NFR6: Desktop application interface shall be intuitive and easy to navigate.
NFR7: Simulations shall provide guidance during interactions.

4.4 Portability Requirements
NFR8: The system shall support compatibility with Oculus Rift Headset.

Project: AutoMorph - Driver Safety Data Augmentation via Diffusion Models
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This document provides an overview of AutoMorph, a project aimed at improving driver safety systems by generating synthetic images of underrepresented risky behaviors using diffusion models.

1.2 Scope
The project improves driver monitoring systems by augmenting existing datasets with synthetic images of behaviors like yawning, eyes closing, and altered poses. It uses inpainting, pose augmentation, and text-conditioned generation.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- LDMs: Latent Diffusion Models
- DDIM: Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models
- VAE: Variational Autoencoder
- U-Net: U-Net Architecture

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
AutoMorph extends existing driver monitoring datasets by generating synthetic images to improve the accuracy of AI models in detecting high-risk behaviors, addressing the issue of underrepresented data.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Inpainting to add behaviors (e.g., yawning) to existing images.
- Pose augmentation to modify driver posture.
- Text-based conditioning to guide image generation.
- Seamless dataset integration.

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- AI Developers: Who will integrate the augmented data into driver monitoring systems.

2.4 Operating Environment
Web-based application for dataset integration.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Driver Image Augmentation
FR1.1: The system shall augment existing driver-facing images with underrepresented risky behaviors (yawning, eyes-closing).
FR1.2: The system shall use inpainting techniques to target specific regions for augmentation.

3.2 Pose Augmentation
FR2.1: The system shall modify the posture of the driver using DragDiffusion-like functionality.

3.3 Text-Based Conditioning
FR3.1: The system shall support text-based prompts to guide the image generation process.

3.4 Dataset Integration
FR4.1: The augmented images shall be seamlessly integrated into existing datasets.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Performance
NFR1: The system shall augment at least 20-30% of the existing dataset within a reasonable timeframe.
NFR2: Generated images must closely mimic real-world counterparts.

4.2 Scalability
NFR3: The system shall support scalable generation, handling an increasing number of images or new classes of behaviors.

4.3 Usability
NFR4: The system shall provide an easy-to-use interface for integrating augmented images into existing pipelines.

Project: BalleBaaz - Cricket Management and Engagement System
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This document outlines the requirements for BalleBaaz, a cricket management and engagement system designed to address issues in local cricket systems like biased umpiring, ground booking difficulties, and lack of player visibility.

1.2 Scope
The project develops a system with scoring, stat tracking, ground booking, match/player highlights, a DRS system, AI kit generation, umpire booking, coaching drills, fitness sections, and a community section.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- DRS: Decision Review System
- AI: Artificial Intelligence
- LBW: Leg Before Wicket
- ATS: Applicant Tracking System (mentioned in context of resumes)

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
BalleBaaz aims to bring local cricket systems to the next level by providing a digital platform that streamlines management, ensures fairness, and promotes talent.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- User management for players, clubs, umpires.
- Real-time scoring and stats tracking.
- Ground booking system.
- Match/player highlights generation.
- DRS system for LBW decisions.
- AI kit generation.
- Coaching and drills sections.
- Community section for communication.

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Player: Joins matches, views stats, interacts in community.
- Umpire: Officiates matches, views upcoming games.
- Club Owner: Manages venues and bookings.
- Team Owner: Manages team, books grounds, generates kits.
- Coach: Manages training sessions for players.

2.4 Operating Environment
Web and mobile application.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 User Management
FR1.1: The system shall allow players, clubs, coaches, umpires, and admins to register and log in.
FR1.2: The system shall manage user profiles and statistics.
FR1.3: The system shall tailor functionalities based on user roles.

3.2 Scoring and Stats Tracking
FR2.1: The system shall provide a real-time ball-by-ball scoring interface.
FR2.2: The system shall track and store player and team stats over matches.
FR2.3: The system shall maintain a match history for each player.

3.3 Ground Booking System
FR3.1: The system shall allow users to book venues by choosing ground, date, time, and match type.
FR3.2: The system shall guarantee clash-free scheduling for grounds.

3.4 Match/Player Highlights
FR4.1: The system shall automatically compile key moments (wickets, boundaries) into match highlights.
FR4.2: The system shall generate player-specific highlights.

3.5 DRS System
FR5.1: The system shall apply DRS on recordings for confirming LBW decisions.
FR5.2: The system shall make use of ball tracking and hawk-eye technology.

3.6 AI Kit Generation
FR6.1: The system shall allow users to generate kit designs based on input type and color.

3.7 Umpire Booking

FR11.1: The system shall allow players to search for specific drills and view instructional videos.

emoBot is designed to provide a more empathetic and accessible mental health support tool by integrating advanced AI techniques for understanding user emotions and delivering responses in a preferred format.

- Emotion recognition and mood tracking.

SOFTWARE REQUIREMENTS SPECIFICATION (IEEE 830)
Project: CodeCommClassifier - Automated Code Comment Classification
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This document outlines the problem and proposed solution for CodeCommClassifier, a project aimed at improving code comprehension by classifying source code comments into categories like functionality descriptions, metadata, etc.

1.2 Scope
The research focuses on developing a model to classify code comments with higher accuracy than existing baselines, using advanced transformer models to help developers quickly locate relevant information.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- SVM: Support Vector Machine
- NLP: Natural Language Processing
- BERT: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers
- F1-score: A measure of a model's accuracy

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
The system automates the classification of code comments, helping developers to optimize code comprehension and streamline maintenance efforts by reducing manual searching through extensive documentation.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Dataset acquisition from NLBSE.
- Data preprocessing (cleaning, normalization).
- Feature extraction (TFIDF).
- Oversampling (EDISON) for low-resource languages.
- Model selection and evaluation (CodeBERTa, GraphCodeBERT, PLBART).
- Domain post-training.
- Evaluation metrics (Accuracy, F1-score, Precision, Recall).

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Software Developers: Primary beneficiaries who need to quickly access relevant comments.
- Open-Source Communities: Who will benefit from better documented code.
- Organizations: Involved in software maintenance.

2.4 Operating Environment
Tool intended for integration into software development workflows.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Dataset Acquisition
FR1.1: The system shall acquire a large corpus of code comments from the NLBSE Tool Competition Website.

3.2 Preprocessing
FR2.1: The system shall remove null spaces and special characters from the raw data.
FR2.2: The system shall convert text to lowercase.
FR2.3: The system shall split the cleaned dataset into training and testing sets.

3.3 Feature Extraction
FR3.1: The system shall extract features by converting code comments into vectorized representations (e.g., TFIDF).

3.4 Oversampling for Low-Resource Languages
FR4.1: The system shall apply the EDISON oversampling technique to balance datasets, especially for low-resource languages like Pharo.

3.5 Model Selection and Evaluation
FR5.1: The system shall experiment with multiple transformer-based models (CodeBERTa, GraphCodeBERT, PLBART).
FR5.2: The system shall evaluate each model's performance on the validation dataset.

3.6 Training the Selected Model
FR6.1: The system shall optimize model performance by tuning parameters (batch size, learning rate, epochs).
FR6.2: The system shall use early stopping to prevent overfitting.
FR6.3: The system shall apply hyperparameter optimization.
FR6.4: The system shall employ multi-level layer aggregation to capture complex patterns.

3.7 Domain Post-Training
FR7.1: The system shall conduct domain post-training to specialize the model in code comment classification.

3.8 Evaluation Metrics
FR8.1: The system shall use Accuracy, F1-score, Precision, and Recall to evaluate model performance.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Performance
NFR1: The system shall process and classify comments within a reasonable time frame.
NFR2: The system shall handle large datasets efficiently.

4.2 Accuracy
NFR3: The system shall aim to achieve classification accuracy higher than the baseline.

4.3 Robustness
NFR4: The system shall be robust to variations in comment styles and programming languages.

Project: HoshyarInvestAI - AI-Powered Investment Platform for Pakistan's Tech Sector
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This document outlines the requirements for HoshyarInvestAI, an AI-powered investment platform designed to help investors in Pakistan's tech sector by providing real-time market insights, sentiment analysis, and risk management tools.

1.2 Scope
The project aims to collect and integrate financial data, public opinion, and trends to provide users with buy, hold, and sell investment signals. It includes modules for data collection, feature engineering, analysis and modeling, a dashboard, and backtesting.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- AI: Artificial Intelligence
- NLP: Natural Language Processing
- PSX: Pakistan Stock Exchange
- TFT: Temporal Fusion Transformer
- RL: Reinforcement Learning
- EGARCH: Exponential GARCH model

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
HoshyarInvestAI bridges the gap between traditional investment models and the tech industry by factoring in innovation, scale, and positioning to provide actionable insights for investors.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Data collection from PSX, financial reports, and news.
- Feature engineering for model training.
- Advanced analysis using TFT, RL, and EGARCH models.
- User dashboard for portfolio monitoring.
- Backtesting for strategy validation.

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Investor: Tech investors in Pakistan seeking data-driven decisions.
- Admin: Manages system performance and user access.
- Data Analyst: Interprets data and refines investment strategies.

2.4 Operating Environment
Web and mobile application.

3. FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
3.1 Module 1: Data Collection and Integration
FR1.1: The system shall gather real-time stock market data from PSX.
FR1.2: The system shall gather financial reports from publicly listed companies.
FR1.3: The system shall analyze market sentiment using news and journal sources.
FR1.4: The system shall integrate world market trends and economic indicators.

3.2 Module 2: Feature Engineering
FR2.1: The system shall preprocess raw stock market and financial data.
FR2.2: The system shall extract relevant features for machine learning models.
FR2.3: The system shall allow feature selection based on investment strategies.
FR2.4: The system shall encode and scale the data for model optimization.
FR2.5: The system shall validate features through backtesting and simulation.

3.3 Module 3: Advanced Analysis and Modeling
FR3.1: The system shall utilize deep learning models to predict stock price movements.
FR3.2: The system shall generate a detailed risk assessment report for each stock.
FR3.3: The system shall identify critical financial trends for tech companies.
FR3.4: The system shall provide recommendations based on risk level.

3.4 Module 4: Dashboard and User Interface
FR4.1: The system shall visualize stock performance in a dynamic dashboard.
FR4.2: The system shall allow users to monitor their investment portfolio performance.
FR4.3: The system shall provide comparison options between different tech companies.
FR4.4: The system shall allow real-time interaction with market data.

3.5 Module 5: Backtesting and Strategy Validation
FR5.1: The system shall backtest historical stock data to validate investment strategies.
FR5.2: The system shall provide users with suggestions for strategy improvement.
FR5.3: The system shall simulate different market scenarios to assess strategy effectiveness.

4. NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS
4.1 Reliability
NFR1: The system shall have a Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of at least 1000 hours.
NFR2: The system shall have failover mechanisms to recover data within 1 hour of failure.

4.2 Usability
NFR3: The system shall allow users to view stock data with a single click.
NFR4: The user interface shall require less than 10 minutes of training for new users.
NFR5: The system shall have built-in error recovery mechanisms for incorrect inputs.

4.3 Performance
NFR6: The system shall respond to user queries (e.g., stock trend analysis) within 3 seconds.
NFR7: The system shall support up to 500 simultaneous user queries without performance degradation.

4.4 Security
NFR8: The system shall encrypt all financial data in transit and at rest using AES-256 encryption.
NFR9: The system shall require multi-factor authentication for all user logins.
NFR10: The system shall log all user activities and notify the admin of suspicious behavior.

- 3D university environment.
- Level progression from easy to hard.

- Interactive mini-map.

- User authentication and leaderboard.

NFR12: The game shall include a user consent policy regarding age and motion sickness.

Project: SmartPark - IoT Based Smart Parking Management System
================================================================================

This document provides an overview of the IoT Based Smart Parking Management System, which aims to enhance user experience by providing a seamless car parking solution using IoT technology.

The project involves installing IoT sensors to monitor parking spaces and developing a mobile application where users can view available slots, make reservations, and handle payments. It includes database integration with Firebase and a pilot deployment.

- MTBF: Mean Time Between Failures

The system maximizes the use of existing parking spaces by introducing a mobile app that monitors availability, allows reservations, and processes payments, eliminating the hassle of traditional methods.

- Real-time slot monitoring via mobile app.

2.4 Operating Environment
Mobile application with cloud-based backend (Firebase).

NFR6: The system shall update parking slot availability within 5 seconds of a car entering/leaving.
NFR7: The mobile app shall load parking availability within 3 seconds.
NFR8: The database shall handle up to 1,000 parking spots in the initial phase.

Project: ARch360
================================================================================

1. INTRODUCTION
1.1 Purpose
This document officially presents ARch360, a new augmented reality solution aimed at complementing the architectural design process, facilitating communication between clients and architects through immersive visualizations in 3D for construction projects.

1.2 Scope
ARch360 uses AR technology (Unity and AI) to turn conventional architectural drawings into 1:1 scale interactive 3D holograms. Clients can navigate virtual representations of future homes or commercial spaces, interact with designs (wall colors, tile designs), and reduce uncertainty about design choices.

1.3 Definitions, Acronyms, Abbreviations
- AR: Augmented Reality
- AI: Artificial Intelligence
- CAD: Computer-Aided Design
- FPS: Frames Per Second

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION
2.1 Product Perspective
The application bridges communication gaps between clients and professionals, reducing misunderstandings and costly revisions during construction phases.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Building instantiation from 3D models (AutoCAD files)
- Area analysis using ARCore
- Wall and floor customization
- Virtual building tours with first-person controller
- AI-powered design suggestions

- API: Application Programming Interface

2. OVERALL DESCRIPTION

CodeNarrator helps developers who often neglect documentation due to time constraints. It ensures both high-level (README) and low-level (code comments) documentation are current and accessible.

2.2 Product Functions (Summary)
- Automatic README generation based on code structure and dependencies.
- Dynamic README updates based on codebase and commit history.
- Automatic addition of pertinent code comments.
- Seamless GitHub integration.

2.3 User Classes and Characteristics
- Developers: Need to update documentation frequently.
- Development Teams: Need consistent documentation for collaboration.
- Open-Source Contributors: Rely on clear README files.

Integrates with GitHub; accessed via a web plugin/UI.

3.1 Data Collection and Filtering
FR1.1: The system shall retrieve repository information from GitHub.
FR1.2: The system shall filter data based on file size, PRs, README, or commit frequency.

FR3.1: The system shall extract comments from the code in the repository.

3.4 Fine-Tuning the NLP Model (CodeBERT)
FR4.1: The system shall fine-tune the CodeBERT model using the preprocessed data.

3.5 Code and README Dissection
FR5.1: The system shall map function calls for use in call graphs.
FR5.2: The system shall track code inputs and outputs.
FR5.3: The system shall generate call graphs.

3.6 NLP Model Output Generation
FR6.1: The system shall fine-tune the CodeBERT model based on dissected features.
FR6.2: The system shall generate a new README file.
FR6.3: The system shall generate meaningful code comments.

3.7 Dynamic Updation
FR7.1: The system shall dynamically update README files and comments based on committed changes.

3.8 UI Development
FR8.1: The system shall design an interactive plugin for end-users.
FR8.2: The system shall develop a user-friendly interface.

3.9 Integration and Deployment
FR9.1: The system shall reintegrate generated results (README, comments) into GitHub.

NFR4: The system shall provide clear error messages.
NFR5: The system shall allow users to generate or change documentation with a single interaction.

4.3 Reliability
